caption: Cover page
Title page
Correlation Summary from August 8, 1962, explaining that this is a summer of references to Armstrong in the FBI file.
Four short references to Armstrong dated 1944, 1948, 1950, and 1951, with redactions.

A letter from Paris inviting Louis to take part in a "Congress of Scholars of the Negro World" in 1956, as well as an article about George Murphy trying to get Armstrong to perform at President Eisenhower's inauguration in 1957.

Three items from 1957, one about a bomb being thrown at Armstrong's concert in Knoxville, Tennessee, and two responses to Armstrong's criticism of the government over the way they handled the Little Rock Central High School integration crisis.
Two items relating to the aftermath of Armstrong's Little Rock comments
Item about Armstrong being invited to the Soviet embassy during a trip to Washington D. C. in 1958. Rest of the page is redacted.
One item about  the Persian Service of USIA carrying a feature on Armstrong, and another item about Armstrong planning to take his band to Yugoslavia and Poland in 1959.
Multiple articles from 1955 and 1960 about Armstrong's tours of Africa and Europe and the possibility of him going to Russia.
Listing of articles referencing Armstrong's 1957 Little Rock comments
A poem, "Mr. Dolly Himself," with a handwritten note addressed to J. Edgar Hoover.
Two short letters addressed to J. Edgar Hoover, praising Armstrong.
Letter to Robert Mose, proposing a Louis Armstrong Day at the 1964 World's Fair.
Letter to disc jockey Fred Robbins including a poem about Armstrong.
List of countries.
Letter from J. Edgar Hoover, in reply to previous letters concerning Armstrong's many accomplishments.
Memorandum to the director of FBI reporting $30,000 dollars of jewelry stolen from the Armstrongs in Los Angeles in 1970.

Memorandum to the director of FBI reporting $30,000 dollars of jewelry stolen from the Armstrongs in Los Angeles in 1970. This is a continuation of the previous page, with more details about the crime and the investigation.
Letter requesting FBI name check on Louis from Mildred Stegall in 1965.
Memorandum from Jack Valenti, assistant to President Lyndon Johnson, requesting a name check and biography on Louis Armstrong. Sent through Mildred Stegall.
Short biography of Armstrong.
Memorandum from Jack Valenti, assistant to President Lyndon Johnson, requesting a name check on Louis Armstrong. Sent through Mildred Stegall.
Three-page teletype discussing Jack Ruby's murder of Lee Harvey Oswald, mentioning Armstrong in relation to his vocalist at the time Jewel Brown, and his manager, Joe Glaser, each of whom knew Ruby.
Three-page teletype discussing Jack Ruby's murder of Lee Harvey Oswald, mentioning Armstrong in relation to his vocalist at the time Jewel Brown, and his manager, Joe Glaser, each of whom knew Ruby.
Three-page teletype discussing Jack Ruby's murder of Lee Harvey Oswald, mentioning Armstrong in relation to his vocalist at the time Jewel Brown, and his manager, Joe Glaser, each of whom knew Ruby.
